speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's quite a story. That would be interesting too. It was to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: How did it come about?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I wrote to people. They sent me a ticket. A farmer, their family was Nelson. They come from my hometown in Sweden. That was years ago. The farm they had a homestead. They took a homestead there in Minnesota. Clay County. It's just out of about 30 miles east of Moorhead. Between Moorhead and Lick Park. It's called Winnipeg Junction. There was a brand line up to Manitoba in Canada on the NP Road. Northern Pacific. The funny part of it was, I stayed a week in Norway in Christiania. Now they call that town Oslo. It was Christiania then. That was in 1905.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I stayed a week there. We got on the boat and we went over to Hull, England. Then on the train to Glasgow, Scotland. I waited there a week. Then we were on the boat, I guess, 9 or 10 days to get into New York. Got on the train there and we come to Minneapolis, Minnesota. That was around with the kids. More kids are there and lots of sweets. Meet a lot of them when they get off the train.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We didn't get on the train until about midnight again. I thought I could stay awake. I did until long. We'd get over to a junction where I was to get off. They were supposed to meet me at 5 o 'clock in the morning. Here I was sound asleep. Nobody woke me up. I woke up and there was daylight. It was all just like it is here. The train was standing still and I looked up and I could see far ago Andy. North Dakota. Here I was, just coming over, couldn't talk to anybody. I sat still and I couldn't get off. The train started again. Here they come, the conductor, looking for a ticket. They took mine the night before but they didn't wake me up. I was sitting there and I said, they're not sleeping. So he stopped the train and kicked me off. I got my little handbag and I got off. There was no use to go after the train. I had sense enough to go back and I walked back to the depot.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Those red caps, you know, around there was an eager black one. He tried everything he could to talk to me. I couldn't understand. Finally he talked Norwegian. Boy, then I was at home. I couldn't understand that all night. So he helped me then. He asked me if I had breakfast and I told him no. I hadn't had nothing since the day before.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He said, you got any money?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I told him yes, I had a little money. He helped me in there. He had a bean to eat at the station. He got ham and eggs and potatoes and toast and everything. He said, now you wait right here until the train comes a little after nine. You know that morning?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Here comes the train going east. He helped me get a ticket on that. So at eleven o 'clock that day, you see the coming from the east at five o 'clock, I come into that station at eleven o 'clock from the west. Nobody did. I got here for the first experience. That's the first one. That was a pretty good experience right there. I was wondering how the hell this was going to become of me. I was all alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: How old were you then?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, you see, that was in five. I must have been nineteen. Just about.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What did you heard about America before you came over that made you want to come?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, that was the old years, them days. You know, they migrated a lot from all the countries over here. This country was new, you know. It wasn't like it is now. Take seventy years from back, you know, this country was new. So in 1989, you know, Spokane burnt up. And so, you know, that's been built since 1989. You can see Spokane now. There is one example what the changes have been here, right here, where we're sitting. When I come here in the seventh, I put in two years. In Minnesota and Wisconsin, Dakota, knocked around. Had a good time. Bad time, good times.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What did you do?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I done every damn thing anybody could think of. I was capable of doing most anything, no matter what I tried, I could do it. I used to drive team. I worked on a farm. I worked with that farmer.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: From there, I think I got there the ninth day of June. And I worked there till the plowing was done in the fall for the ticket. The ticket cost, I think it paid sixty-five dollars to get me over. Damn days. And I worked June, July, August, September, October, and into November. So there were two plowing and everything. So there was nothing more to do, only take the shores on the farm. So I asked him if he was satisfied, I'd go out and work. He said he was. But he wanted me to stay. That's where I made my first mistake. I should have stayed. When I went to school, I could have went to school then. I went there and stayed with him. But I couldn't see that. I didn't know whether. I hadn't seen anything on that farm. So I couldn't tie up on that farm all my life. There I made the first mistake.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Where did you go then?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I went out. First I started in on the N .P. railroad. They were building a new line there. They had helpers there. They elevated the track between Hawley and Lick Park. So past the farm there, they put in a field across that canyon seventy feet higher. That took off somewhat great. They didn't need any helpers anymore on the trains. They could come right straight through from Fargo to St. Paul without helpers. They had a hill there to go up. So they had to send a helper with them always. My God, that took that off. They raised it up seventy feet. That's quite a bit on the railroad. And they could distribute that down forty miles. So they almost come on the level. I worked there a while on the steam shovel. It was night. That's where I met that big Irishman that I bought a bottle of whiskey and gave him. And he drank all of it. I didn't get a drop. I wouldn't get a drink of it. He took it all.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember this guy's name?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I don't. No, I couldn't. By God, I don't know. Oh, it doesn't matter. That was in 1905. That's a long time ago. No, I can't. I can't pick him up.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But you never forgot he drank that whole bottle of whiskey. I don't know. But I didn't buy him any more. And I walked six miles down to town and got there and brought it up. I thought I'd be a good fellow. It was cold in November and December in the fall. It was cold in that country. So when we were going in for dinner, I thought, good drinking. All we eat, we just write. He appreciated it. He said, Gug-gug-gug-gug-gug. He said, the lad, that was a good drink. He took it all. And you could never see it. We even faced him a bit. Better with me, I'd have been sleeping under that steam fable.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: So where was the farm that you worked on and didn't go back to?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It was there at Winnipeg Junction. This is where I worked then at the camp. It was only a mile from the farm. Part of that railroad come across the farm. That new railroad. Well, the old one did too. The old line did too down in the bottom of the ravine or creek.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And it did a better swing. Curved around it, you know, come on across. Hit the side hill and went up to Lake Park. And that took out that trail 70 feet. But you know, on the rail, that's quite a bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: So then, where did you go then?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, after that I went up to Duluth, Superior, Wisconsin. I was up around Hibbing, up in the Iron Belt. And I was out to Akeley, Red River.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Day later, I worked for them in the woods first and then at the mill. $180 a day. Ten hours, piling lumber.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, I worked in the woods there. No, we went out in the woods, there were four of us. And we hired out for $35 a month and bored.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But if you quit, you see, you get a dollar a day. For the day you work, you see. So then, what you had to get in order to get the $35 a month, you had to stay till the job was done. And I don't know if it's done yet. As long as there's any timber, you don't have to have that company working. You never got through. But that's the way they hired you.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: So when you left, you lost that money?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, no, we got a dollar a day. A dollar and bored. For each day we worked, you see. But we didn't get that $35. We got $26. Twenty-six working days.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, we were bad. Then from there I went to Dakota. Oh, there I had another experience. That was pretty good, too. We worked on the, we've been up on the Mesabi North, there in the Iron Belt up near Eben, Minnesota. There was another kid there. He was older than me. He was born at Proctor out of Duluth. His folks lived there. And you know, we got into town and he was broke the first evening.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They say, three weeks, two weeks, three weeks, get $15, $20, $20. Into town, blow it and come back again. So there was, I went with him one night and we were getting around the saloon. And when I was in hell, I didn't get even the room. Hard little, I was broke. They just kept the bum and the hell out of you, you know. Around the saloons. So the next time I come in, so I got over and got myself a room and I said, Dude, I was out gone. Next morning I looked somewhere and they said they were broke and didn't have any money. And I said, No, I ain't got any money.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What happened to you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I said, I happened to be over there and I said, It went. But I had my little dollars. Not very many, but I had a few. So that day I picked on this fellow to go with me and I went and looked over the boards and they sent people out to Leeds, Montana on the Great North to build a reservoir. They were building a reservoir. They had to work on the dump. That's where they built this dam across the canyon to hold the water for the locomotives to get water.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So, we got as far as we drove that first night and the next day when we got into Laramie, I think, in North Dakota. And I was going over and I could see they were building a dam there. So I told them, His name was Charlie. And I said, Let's get off here. And I said, It's the same work we hired out for, same road. Let's get off and see if we might get on there. The other day, that was in North Dakota. So we did. We got off and we walked back to this camp there and they said, Sure. But there was no trouble. We'll get on. So we got our little pack sack with us and came out to camp that night in that chopper. We got our bed fixed up. We had to work nights, you know. And they come in there with the train load of dump cars, you know. The dirt to make this field was about 80 feet high, the whole water. And there was frost chunks in there, you know. The frost go down in that country down about four feet. And they dug that with them big steam shovels, you know. And there was chunks coming in there four foot square or more. And they put them in the 20 yard dump cars, you know, on the rail. And they loaded them so all you had to do was knock the pin out of them and they dump. And this was in April and it was still cold, frosty. So I tripped one, you know, in one of them cars. And they had it loaded so it would go there and only half of it fell out. And the car come back again. Now, damn, we had got caught under it. So I went over to my partner and I said, I think we ought to get out of here before I get killed in it. Oh, he said, kid, he said, he was a little older than me. He said, we'll stay and we'll get a few dollars. So I said, okay. We went. By God, you know, we had after dinner another train come in, you know. There were 30, 40 box cars or dump cars in the train. And they kept shoving it with the locomotive's part of me, see. And we kept tripping and there were three of us on there. So we got pretty close together there one time. And five of them damn cars tipped towards us. We put in there and gathered all of us. So then my partner said, oh, he said, I think you were right. Let's get there, Lord. So we did. And, you know, next day we could have fired out on the farmers there, 40 dollars a month. And bored. But only one. It was nice, you know, in April that sun was shining. That was a regular breather, you know, that day, I guess. So we thought we'd wait another day. The farmer was pulling out on a coat, telling them we want to go out. So then we said, no, we'll wait till we get there. We were both on the same farm. Next day come here was a God-torn blessed. And it lasted a week. There was no farmers inside, no place, and no work. So, and then what little money I had, you know, that was gone. He didn't have a cent when he left Duluth. So I was feeding them, bored in the room, you know, and we had a little snort here and there. They went, so we were, then we were clean. So one morning we took out, we heard about a railroad starting out from Devil's Lake, North Dakota, into Casselton, toward Fargo, on the Great Northern Branch line. Then past Devil's Lake, which is a lake out of the town of Devil's Lake, and it's a lake out there, Devil's Lake, too, on the reservation, the Indian reservation. So we hiked 45 miles that day and no breakfast. And we got over into town, you know, fairly early in the afternoon. There was no grass growing on the raw feet. We were hiking, and fast, too. Had a good early start. The contractors were in the yard there, you know, in the tent, set up, little office. Their equipment was coming in. So we split up so we could cover more ground and maybe get a job. And I met up with two brothers, the Wilson brothers. They were both Great Northern Civil Engineers, you know, they were Indianers, both of them, but they were stuck in the job. And I guess for some reason, I guess, what little I could talk, I used what I could. I couldn't talk very much. One of my needs is a job, pretty bad. So they said, you go up town and stay. And they said, just as soon as we get the horses, we got the equipment here, but we're waiting for horses to come in. And you can get on with us. So I told them I would, but I said, I can't stay. I said, I got to have a job. And they said, why?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I said, I haven't got a cent, only what I, nothing, only what I got on my back. I'm broke. I said, I'll take you up to town, I'll give you the room. Well, I said, still that's fine, but I said, I got a partner there, too. I said, you'd better abandon me if you could take on both of us. I said, okay, we'll wait, we'll be here. We said, can you find him?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I told him, we split up so we could cover all of them and see if we couldn't get on. Thank God, he said, I don't know, we went and found him. He took both of us in, got us uptown and got us a room and three meals a day and we waited there ten days. And we stayed out, went out with him and we stayed till his job was done. We never went into town, never left the camp room that day, we started to leave. Till we helped him with all the equipment back into town again and loaded on the cars. And he never mentioned what he paid us. We got forty dollars a month on board.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So, it was lucky.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: How long did that last?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, that till in July. That's about the, see in April, May, June into July. That was about a four month job. We had so many miles to know that. And on level ground, we had this one dump there, I guess about thirty-five foot fill. Across the little ravine. All horse work, scraper, plow and scraper. So we do teams.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Worked with the horses. Then I went back to the, I was an old farmer, I helped them through the harvest. I told them the year before I'd be back. So there in July, you know, I was back there in August. Started in cutting the wheat. And I helped them through the harvest. Went back up around the loot again. Till next spring, I come out here and worked on the McGoldrick. I come out here in April. Worked for McGoldrick Lumber Company. You heard of him?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: McGoldrick, I think he's a boy. Oh, he used to be the president of the commerce people there in town. Long time here. But the old man is dead. He was a nice old man. I've heard him several times. He was a nice old man. They logged all that country on Tencent.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know where you go over from?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was McGoldrick's outfit. They had railroads all over there in from Tencent. And up to the corner there where you get into Idaho. Over the hill where the highway goes. He loved that country. I went through there when the railroads in there. His railroads come out of Tico. That's where he landed the logs on the Union Pacific. And brought them into Spokane. And over the hill there towards Santa was... Blackwell.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They were on Santa Creek.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Over through... What is that, a mine?

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: So you worked then around Tencent?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, no. I never worked that only just going through. No, I never worked for McGoldrick in the woods. He'd come up and see us at Potlatch a lot of times. Old man. J .P. McGoldrick. He was a nice old man. Hell of a nice fellow. Just as simple as any of us lumberjacks. I had run the camp out of Clark. He had one over two years. Thirty-nine and forty. Forty-one. There were three hundred and fifty men in that camp. That's a big camp. We had twenty-four skiddin' cats and an old dozer.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I had three assistants.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oscar Agram, the one there in Clark Lancaster. I don't know, I guess Clark is dead now, I think. And Bill Greenwood is dead. They were my assistants.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had three locomotives. And twenty-five cats. Twenty-four skiddin' cats and a little old dozer. That went to the nine forty.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What camp was this?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was camp thirty-five.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Was that a car camp?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes. That was the car camp there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The rutlets had started the grade in there. Which we furnished up after the consoler did. After they merged the rutlets and rutlets and clay water. That's before the rutlets' porters started in.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There was a rutlets timber company then.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Before they got the fire on Marbury Creek, before they put in the incline. But they never used it. That timber was tipped in there. Went in there and they had a spur there and I put in a wire so we could turn the locomotives. And inside of that wire I put that whole damn camp. We had camp cars, bunk cars enough there for three hundred and fifty men to sleep. Cook cars. We had two, three dining cars and a cook car. And the rail, we were sittin' in that wire. Rail all around us. In the pocket, just a little pocket in the hill there. I like to walk there sometime and see it. I think the road goes in there too. I think the Forest Service used the old railroad now for their truckload. Forest Service. Somebody told me that that's the biggest car camp since the Panama Canal was built. I don't know. It was quite a job to get that in and get, you only had so much room, you see. And to build that and get it in there, by God, I had an extra damn car, you know, the length of it. We figured them out and get them set so they'd all fit in. We had a, I set the office car first. That's the first one we set. And then the storeroom. And then the kitchen car. And then the dining car. And then we put four bunk cars. And we cut off that track and slid it over and set another one in. And I put in two bunk cars, no, two bunk cars and a dining car. And that dining car set right across from the kitchen. A big one, a long, I think it was seventy foot. And then filled out with bunk cars again and then put in a third track and a fourth track. Then we had a little space in there and I put in the, we had a light plant, you know, and showers and a washroom. And a filing car, which for the South Islanders to see. We had a little corner left, not let damn two cars in. That took up all the space there was. But that was quite a bit of figuring before I could get it in and get. And the rails, we just cut the rails, you know, and drove it in and cut it off. And finally we got the main line to go by it and the wire around it and the main line on the other side going up.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: One dining car was enough for everybody? We had one for each track?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had two, two big ones. And that co-car was around twenty-four hours. See the mechanics there worked on nights. Had to, you know, because we had all the machines out in the daytime. So they had to work nights in order to get them ready in the morning. Had a machine truck brought in, we had that on rails too. Had a lathe and a drill press and a steam hammer and

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: a planer and old Ernie Schmidt was there, he was there, he was there, the machinist. Any damn thing you could give him but don't, you can tell him and he can make it. But if you mark it down on the paper he was stuck. He couldn't do it. But just talk to him and tell him and by God he made it up. Actually I get it, I get it, I got it. Or bring in a bolt or bring in something and here it is. Needed quite a few of them, five of them. But don't, if you mark it down on the paper and draw a picture of it he won't do it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He was a good old fellow, I like that old Schmidt, he was dandy.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He was good with the steam, he was good. Any damn thing on those Merrions, we had Merrion loaders, you know. They were built in Ohio I guess. In locomotives, you know, the lima shades and little rod engines, that was an American belt. And a Heisler, wherever they come from. But he could fix any of them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I had two camps there, in the spring we started with another one over the hill, camp 37. Over on Bussel Creek. And we talked, we talked over the hill. Up the hill and down the hill till we got onto the railroads and loaded them. So we had the two camps then. But they got through, we got, that's pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Was it much tougher to plan for a big camp like that than the usual camp? Were you planning for the supply and everything?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, not the supply, that was, that was, because we just call in a lot of times, you know. We call in to Spokane, call in in the evening and you get it back out on the train the next day. The clock, that was all right. For the material, for the caterpillars, you know, the cats and the dozer, that was fine. We could call in, call them and you know, by God, on the next train they'd have it. Sometimes we had to go to Mary's and get it. Run down, a lot of times I'd run down to Mary's in the night and meet the train. And the crew was working there, you know, and I'd be back in a couple hours, three. And what they needed in the morning, the cat was out working.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, they had, when the first supply, you know, they had, for groceries, they had the warehouse in Bowell. And you just call in, send in your orders, no call, we had phones. Call in, next day they'd talk them out with, whole truckload of, they took a lot of groceries for 350 men. Truckload a day, putting it in.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Was it hard to keep track of what all the guys were doing with that many men?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It just seemed like a huge size camp to have at one time. Well, I guess, I didn't have no trouble that way.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I could put in there, tell you that, where every one of them was, any time of the day. I could tell you where they were, or who it was. And we were scattered on three, two, three sections of land.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, that part of it was, I didn't have no trouble, but. Well, it sounds like for you, you had no trouble at all with that big camp then. No, I, for my own self, I didn't have any, no, I didn't have no trouble. I could get, I got by with them fine. For some reason, I don't know why.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: How big an area did they log out of that camp? What was the area around that they finally logged off?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, that must be, I think we had, it must have been three miles up to the, the wide, somewhere around that two, three mile circle.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That'll take in five, six sections or more. You know how big a section is, a mile square. So, it took in quite a little bit of tattooing.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's what I told the boss one time, Billings, he, he asked me one time if, he said, don't you ever get tired of looking at the same old faces.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know if I could answer that or not, but I said, I'll put it this way, I tell you. You, you put your foot in my shoes. I said, we're standing here, and I said, here, I'm getting out here in the west hood, in front of camp 30. Or I think it was up on Mason Meadow.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I said, I want to, I got to be able to, when I get out here and look from here to Bowell, that's been about 60 miles. I said, I got man strung out the whole length of that. And I said, there's four train crews, and five or six section crews, and then we're getting close to camp, and I said, here's camp 32 down there. And this is 34 up here. And I said, I got, when I get out here in the morning, I got to be able to see all of them, all of it, in my own mind. And I got to know where they are. And I said, you know, in the air, I said, in the morning, there is three, four saw gang come along. They want a new job, and I tell them, got the strips all marked out, skidways all built, and then go down to strip 81 and 37, whatever it is, it's marked on spursaw and so. And I said, they're off, they're gone. And I tell them, I'll see you sometime today when I get around to you. If I don't get to today, I'll get to you tomorrow, but I'll be with you. And I said, go from one to the other, and they're gone, here come a three, four team stitch, they want a new strip. Tell them the same thing, the logs are all cut, the skidway is in, go on down to strip number so and so and go to work. I'll see you sometime in the day. And I said, in about ten minutes, they're all gone, and I said, then that's just me and the barn boss and the blacksmith. And I said, there are only three left here, and I said, it only takes me about fifteen minutes, and they're gone. Now I said, if I had a new cruise, if I had a new cruise, those old faces look pretty good. And I said, they believe in me, and I know them, they know me, and I said, they believe in me, and I swear on to them. I said, by God, they take my word for it, and they go to work. I said, that's the only explanation I can give you. And I said, whether it's a button in the cap or a kick in the butt, I don't know whatever it is that's answered. He said, I was wondering, he said, you always have the biggest crew of any of our formats. And he said, you have the least turnover than any of them. And I said, you can come here anytime, I said, from Monday to Saturday, I don't care any day in the week. And I said, you'll never find a man sitting around the camp there waiting for the foreman. I said, I'm always waiting on them. I'm waiting on them. I said, I got them. Then nobody's sitting around here, they're all working. And he said, I often wonder how. Well, that was it. I never had any trouble with the crews, which come in pretty good. Seemed to get along pretty damn good. Oh, nobody can suit 200, 300 men in the camp. That is, somebody got the old sign of the bitch or something, you know. And you know that's the answer. That's the nature of Little Umbrella, I can't know it. Because you don't pay no attention to it. That's all in a day's work, you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, you said that you went to work for this guy in Spokane, the guy that... McGoldrick. McGoldrick.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: And then after that, is that when you first came down to Potlatch?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, I think. Yes. We worked there until the Fourth of July. Come, and you know kids now, kids then, was no better than kids now or no worse either, I guess. We were all kids. And we got into town, you know, too much to see, too many places to go. And we worked every day, ten hours a day, piling lumber. And nights, you know, we stayed at the Inland Hotel, and that's the closest one we could get to. Get to the mill. I think it's on the...used to be on the corner of the vision and...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: ...the front street entrance now. I believe that was it. I'm not sure whether... But I think that was the place, or else it's the next block up there, I don't know. But as we closed, we could get to the place of work, you see. We had to walk, walk to work every morning, you know. But, you know, we had another time, and so when the end of the month come, you know, we had no money. That was all gone as we went. We had paid every two weeks. Two weeks gone, and so was our money. The next payday. So I, me and another fellow, we took a trip up to Wallace, went up to Mullen, up to the Snow Star Mine, and I went down in the hole there half a mile. Walked up to the inlet, to this shaft, you know. They come up, they had a little cage, you know, to let you down. And I got down in there, and I said, by God, I'll soon, soon, quick enough, get under there. So I said, I'm gonna get the hell out of here. It's too deep, I know it. So I got up, and we were three of us then. So I hiked downtown, and there was a boat now just right by the railroad track up Mullen. And I was sitting in there eating dinner, here come the other two. They took the next kids up. None of us stayed there. So that was my mining. I never tried again. When I got down there half a mile, that's a long ways down. 2 ,600 feet down. That's deep, ain't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Let me try that. And when we come back down again, and we tried on the, they built the Milwaukee Road. We went out till the end on the railroad down in Milwaukee. We didn't stay there just overnight. Hiked back again, come in to Spokane. And knocked around a little bit, we come into Spokane. I said, I'm gonna get the slowest wages I can find on the board. So there was employment offices all over the, on Trent and all the streets coming to. There was Front Street then, where the Milwaukee Depot was built in 1908.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I bought last pay at the least of any of them, and I said, that's where I'm going. I'm going to take that. I mean, if I don't run around anymore. We worked in the yard there, it was in fall of seven. And that fall again, we just, after we got through in the river there, I told you up in there before, we just crept up and grabbed a lot of the river bottom for ballasting the yard tracks through the lumber yard. In potlatch. And we worked in that water up under our arms, drove a team, fell our own slip, just take the foot on it. And they come up, the day goes first, you know, holding it. Italians were holding the slip, and we had a hell of a time to keep those shovelers going. We had to pull that up on the bank, you know, so they could load them on the cars and take them up in the yard. And we worked like hell, you know, turning them horses round and round and round, and we never got nowhere. So the boys come around there one day, and I asked them, his name was Charlie Peterson. I said, Charlie, how much extra will you give us if we hold our own slip and you take those boys with you, take them to hell out of here?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I said, I couldn't. They held you, he said, but I'll go up and see the super. And we'll pay. So he was backing a little bit, and he said, we can't raise you, anybody. He said, we'll give you two hours a day more, we'll give you 12 hours pay instead of 10. I give you 50 cents a day more, you see. And we had two and a half for four, and we got three dollars then. So I said, take them for the with you. After that, you know, we never worked only, oh, we worked about, oh, maybe three hours a day. In the morning we got out there, you know, and we had a sashay, and we hauled up more, three hours. We hauled up more gravel than they could load in two hours. We sat on the bank there, sun was up and nice and dried up for dinner, walked into camp and had dinner. We made another sashay and got plenty of gravel up there, you know, and then we went and sat on the bank, you know, and dried up. When quitting time come, we went, took the horses to the barn, and we stayed there till the ice was in the water. That was too much. You know, you dig in, you had to reach down once in a while, you know, with your arms clean to the shoulders, and we were in to up out our necks, getting gravel. And that ice cold water, you know, that got a little too much for us. Then I went up in the woods on a steel gang, got up to Harvard, laid it up to Bowdoin. That was in seven, fall of seven, when they had the wooden money. You heard of that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It was a panic, you know. The sales were no good, lumber didn't move, and jobs were scarce, money was scarce, and putting in all that potlatch. We issued coupons for the men that all worked around the mill there, you know. They could get coupons and go to the store instead of money to get groceries. Family men, you know, lived there. And up in the woods we got money, gold and silver. First paycheck or two I got there in potlatch was all in gold and silver. Twenty dollar gold piece, tens, fives. Not very many, but what few there was. If we had thirty dollars coming, we got a twenty dollar gold piece and a ten. Thirty dollars in gold. No paper money.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Why did they say wooden money?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They all had coupons. They made them themselves, you know, good at the store only, you see. They, potlatch, they had their own store. And they stayed, money was scarce, you know, so. Well, you know, they issued them, give them those tickets or coupons, and they could go in there and get what they wanted. They were worth a dollar or fifty cents, two bits, just like money, in trade.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I think they were using them, most of the companies used that, because money was scarce. I worked in the Grand Foye, the old Palmer Lumber Company, in nineteen-eight or six months. They paid in gold too, everything. Never saw any bills.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know when the bills come in later.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I guess in twelve, fourteen, I guess they started to come in. Dollar bills and tens and so forth. But before that, you know, they always paid in gold and silver.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Got a pay envelope. There was your time sheet on there, and the days you worked, and the money was in it. They handed you that little envelope, and there was your pay. And the time clip there, you could check it over if you wanted to, the days you worked and how much.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I'm just curious, you say that the lowest pay at Potlatch was what made you want to go there.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: How come? Because the high pay wasn't so good, the jobs?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, there was in the fall, there was seven, when this panic started. And everybody paid, oh they paid two, six bits, three dollars, two and a half. And you come there, and the camps were full. There was no place to, they did let you go to work, but there was no place to stay, no place to eat. So I figured that I had the lowest one. I figured that, of course, nobody go there. They won't go there, they go for the highway, so I did too first time. And go, like you said, there's always green around the other side of the fence or on over. I did follow that too for two, three years there, to run around. And you never had anything, you never stayed any place. Always something wrong, where they paid more wages, there was something wrong, something else. So by God damn it, Potlatch and they lost one. And I stayed with it after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What else did you work at there in 1907, after you left the gravel work?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh God, I done everything that anybody could do in the woods. I was swamping and I was sawing, falling timber, and I chased the donkeys that went there. I done was whistle punk and I said, jokers. And I used to trail there, we had a wire strung from the, hooked up to the whistle on the donkey. And strung out to the back end of the job, you know, they run out. Then when they were short-lined, then they were only half a mile that the cable went out. So I had half a mile of cable, or a wire hung on trees, you know, out there. And fairly tight, and I had a stick, and I hit it, see. And you know, just hit it out there, it goes toot. And it was toot, you know, one was to stop. And two,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: no, one, one, all was stopped for one, when they were running, to stop. And if you want to back up, it was two. And we were standing still. And if you want to go ahead slow, you get three. One, two, three. And just like you hit it on that wire, that whistle goes toot, toot, toot. And there was a spring on that, you know, shut it off, the steam.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Then I had to chase the load in, that half a mile into the landing, and let them through the bull block and in on the landing. That's my job, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Let them through the what?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They had a bull block, and for a lead block, you know, like, the donkey was sitting over that house there. The landing was big enough for the whole tree length to come in, so they could saw it off, you know, that'd be a hundred feet across. And you had to have a lead block there, and then from there on you could take that old anyplace, and we call it a bull block. That's it, the landing, all the logs. Every drag come in to that same morning, and then you change from there on in the circle. So most of the time, you know, you run there, oh, probably 50 trips a day that I've made in and out. Well, you know, that's a lot of hiking. I used to ride the logs, you know, get on the drag and ride on them, but I had to watch that haul back was in there tight. You know, that'd break your damn leg if it'd slip over and hit you. Break you all to pieces, and I used to ride them to me, and then I had to run. Let them through the bull block and then run out, and be out there ahead of that bullock again. Before that come out, and I had to be back there where the crew was working, you know, and stop it. I had to be there with the whistle again. Then when they got through hooking on this money, three, eight or nine, five or six, or eight or nine, three, whatever they could get hold of, you know, through a drag, pull it in, and I had to be back there again. So that kept me pretty messy. Keep you pretty warm, too. Oh, yes, I didn't sweat any. I didn't sweat, but I did. And it was, I kept the elbow out of hiking. I made my elbows there every day, back and forth.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When the drag going on, you had to run to keep up with it. But they got closer to the donkey. When they first started, you know, the line was out, and the drum was pretty small, about foot. And then it peeled up, you know. When they got into the donkey, that was, oh, this pool was that big. Then they go fast. So the farther they got closer to the donkey, the faster the load go.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And the same with the albank, you know, when the main line was in, the albank drum was almost empty. But before the bullock got way out there a half a mile again, then that drum was fast, so that hook would go pretty fast, you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, it was interesting, I tell you, that was the first donkeys that I ever seen. It seemed the first time they ever had them. And that was in seven, they got them and they used them. Oh, the last donkey used to was to be in...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: in thirty-eight or nine. I think that was the last one we had. We had one there, they called it the pot-light special, that had a main drum with a tight line, and a pulling line, and a haulback, and a little, what we call a dolly on the outside for spotting cars with. It had five drums or six, five or six drums on that one. And the pot-light special, I don't think that was the only one they ever built. They built it in Portland.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Do you understand all the difference in the lines?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Pretty well.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Well, what's a tight line?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Tight line, we had a tight line, we set up poles, spire trees or poles, and we had what we call a shoe, that had a groove, so you laid the cable in that. And then you had to figure out when you go over hills. So you go over probably three or four ravines or hills, you know, and get up, and you had a tail all the way up on a hill high. And you had to figure out when you raised them poles, just so when you tighten that line, you had to lay them in there, in each one. You had a little cable, a little 3-8 line we used to just drag along, you know, with kind of a tag line, and lift that cable up and lay it in there, go to the next gen pole and raise it up. And sometimes, one road, I think we had 14 of them on one road a mile long. We had a road in Yavin and a ground line donkey on the outside, a yard for it, and horses along there to haul in and just to haul in with that main donkey. So when you tighten that line, it was little, just curved enough on each one, so she stayed there. That was only a half, she used an inch and a half cable for the tight line. And that was grooved out, you know, just enough to bury half of that cable in it. And we had a trolley carriage walked over there with two wheels on it, two shivs on it, and they were grooved just to go over that cable and stay on it. But no matter where they were, you know, no matter what the pull was, between them and so forth, every one of them, the tight line was tight and pulled down. You had to hold it down or else it would fall off, you know, and then you were done then, you would get another one. Raise it up again because you lost the load and the trolley and the whole work. So that was quite a job to get that set.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And sometimes we get a million feet to the road logs and change line and set it up again. It takes a couple of days to set it up, get a new one, switch it over to a new set.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: How long would it take you to run a million feet, let's say? How many days would it take to run in one place?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, setting, we had one donkey setting that the donkey set there worked 18 months on one setting, just 18 months.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We let him drive that was up out of Vancouver. I paid him $6 .50 and put him on the car. I gave him the donkey set on the car and they unloaded and set it and built the landing as it started. And they sawed the timber and loaded on the car $6 .50. You see there was a crew of 21, I think, in that crew. And they were there 18 months before they finished the job. That was the biggest setting that I had ever seen. That it fit in to get one donkey to last that long. I don't know how many million feet we got in on that, but none of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: How long would most of your lines stretch? How long would a line stretch from a donkey?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The early ones were half a mile, the short line. The short line would go half a mile and the longest one we had was a mile. But then you see, when you set the donkey, when you set the donkey and set the first road, you took the haul back around the whole, all of it, and come in. See, around the whole setting, if there was a section, you know, they haul back around the whole work first. And then pull the main line out with the first road. And then after that, you see, you go like the donkey set here, and you could make a, you take the first road, you had the landing like this, and all you have to do is burr out, so here was the lead and the landing. Then you take the first road like this, but the haul back had to go clean around there, see. You had the first road there, and after that we just dropped off about 400 feet, and switched the line over again, then drop off again like that. They all come into the same, and keep on that way till you come back here again. So you move from one side just clear across to the other. You just made a circle, you see. But you had to get, this was the first one you take out.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's alright.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I can tell you that. Oh, I got a question about these Italians working on that gravel.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: How come they couldn't do the job right? They just didn't know how to do it? Was that it? Did they just go and work hard?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, when you, it's the same, you know, you drive a car. Man standing there to help you, he can't. He can't figure with you. You moving it, and he can't keep up with you. The same with the team. When you drive, you know what you would do if you're, I was driving the team, well I could feel, and I'd set this paper in the ground, you know, and hold it until you're moving ahead, and it just, if the feeling you have in your own self, I'd set the foot down on the handle, you know, just bear down on that, put my weight on it, and up come the front of the scraper, and she was loaded. And when you would drive it in, she wouldn't tip then, because the lead was right in front of her, you see. So after she once come up, you know, she'd pull that thing in. But the idea was to get it started, and when you have, you handle the power when you got the lines and the horses. You got the power in yourself, in your own hand, and you can feel on the scraper what you can do with it. And it set her down too deep, you know, and then she'd tip over with them. Then you had to turn around, turn around, turn around. And it's the same with the steam donkey, I used to run them, spell them off once in a while, and then you go in there, just for the change, for fun. You got hold of the throttle, you held the throttle, you know, and you held the friction, either by hand or with your foot, and you can feel. It's a feeling you get in yourself, and that's something that's pretty hard to explain, but it's a life, the steam is alive in your life. And by God, you know, you can feel what you can do with it, you either open up and give a little more, or you ease up a little bit on that throttle. And the same when you run the locomotive, that's the same thing, it's part of you. Johnson Barrow, that throttle, it's part of you when you sit there in the cab, you know, you have a brake, your ear brake, that with you, and the train behind you, you can feel it, you're feeling, you feel it all the way. You sit there watching, you watch ahead of you, watch behind you, but it's part of you. You understand?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You work into that, and it's, when you open that throttle, it's just as much as your own heart beckon. It's part of you. You feel just, and you can feel just what the machine can do. And you give a little more, sometimes you give too much, but that's it. If you don't get enough, you're stuck, and if you get, sometimes it's a little too big alone, and got in the hole somewhere, and you knew where that was, as you watched the drum, and you could tell just about where you was, and if you had a hard place, you know, well you're not starting the throttle a little wider, so you know it, try to get through it, so the wound gets stuck. Or sometimes you give a little too much, and it was tight, you know, and you break that inch and a half cable.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That will take us, over some time an hour to get it spliced and get it back out, and you once again splice it together.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: How did they splice that cable to get it back together?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They roll them, see, they come in strands, you know, they roll them. And you unravel too, and go, and you unravel too, and take the other end, and roll in with it. One half and one half fall in the same groove, you used to take in 24 foot splice. See that'd be as far from here to that tip. And that'd be a six, twelve or six strand cable. And you're on the road, and then you go the other way again, back and forth, till you got enough to, then you had two spikes, you know, what we call morning spikes, put them through the cable, and open it up, and cut that hemp core out of it, inside. It's a hemp core on old cables, you know, so otherwise they'd wear themselves off from the inside, where the string is on and going to the pulleys and so forth. And then you sink them to ends, bury them. Or sometimes you're nuts, and go under, and go over and under two or three times to tuck the end, and fasten them around an old cable. The wires are broken, the woods are bad, you know, they break. So you'd go in there and open it up in the middle, and just roll the ends in and bury it, so your ends was in the middle of the cable where that little hemp loop was in. And the end of the cable was stuck in there, you know. Oh, that's right, a lot of it. I seen it done on the stage, they used to do it, put it in the twenty, yeah, it's out on the stage there up in Vancouver, BC. They done it in four minutes, put it in twenty-four foot splice. Two on it. On the stage. Two inch cable. In four minutes.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Had the cable there on the stage, stretched out in the two and under, buried in the jade up with good luck and splice too. Dandy.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: When they first brought in the donkeys, the small ones, the ground line, the small ones, did it take a while to get used to how far apart you should be making your, setting them up, would it take time to?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, no I don't think so, I don't think so. Jacks know that. Because you want to get that in your, you see it once and then you know it. Because you had them on the coast not long before we did up there in Idaho. We go out there and take a look and see what they did, and then come up and try to fit it in up in the side hills up there in Idaho. But it was pretty hard to, you take those oak tanders and rigging men that we had in Idaho, there were three of them on the coast. Take a coast man, I've tried a lot of them. Oak tanders come up there, you know, and they were putting us useless. There in that big timber, you see, they had them days, big timber, but we had to set up, they were from Stockland, they said, now okay, here it is. We had a high line and they had high leads, you see. They used to, there was so much more timber on the ground, they could put the railroad up on top of the hill and log up to it. Where in Idaho, the yield is so light, you know, there's so little to the acre in the feetage, so we had to put the railroad in the thick bottom. You couldn't afford to build a road up to the top there. So we had to come downhill and then we had to use a different system and use the high line, you see. We used high lead a little bit, but not much. We couldn't get the railroad up high enough to use it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The high lead is, you have this part right in front of the donkey and you go and anchor your tight line way up on top of the hill there, they only used to go out a thousand feet, 800 to a thousand feet, and we'd go out from half a mile to a mile. And they had no more, just that one spot right there, donkey, that's all. And used no more. But they had a trolley, we'd go on that one line, you know, they could pull the log in. They'd go in from that. Well, we had to, well they used to get one log, you know, with a thousand, two thousand feet in it. We put on fifteen, twenty logs and we only had five, six hundred feet. See the difference?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So it's small timber, you know, in scales. See the difference? Well, how come the hooktenders couldn't do the job?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, we just didn't know how, you know, that, like I said, that took quite a bit of experience and know-how when you go out there and have a mile and you put up eight to ten spire trees or spire poles. We used to raise them on the coast when they put up a spire tree. They went and cut off the top first and hung all the rigging on it and then they fell the tree, put the guidelines on it and fell the tree over into it. And they already sat there. And we really loved them on the garden. You had to get a rig set up and raise it and stick it up. But we had to put in so many, we put in probably ten to their own on that line, you see. And to get them, so you, you know, you go over there for ravines and figure out how high that pole is going to be where you get a little pressure on it. Every one of them had to have a little pressure. From the tail hole into the donkey. If you didn't, well then, there was no good, you see. If you hook on here and tighten this and it raised her feet over the next one, she wouldn't come down on, hit that pole, she'd come on the side of it. Because we had to have a side pole on her too, you see, to hold it. It had to pass her down and sideways. That took quite a bit of, and you know, it didn't know her to do that. So the coachman came in and he was stuck. That was way beyond him. Never seen anything like that. Any man that come from that, one of our men come out there and he was a family that was looking for the first thing. He knew more than the old land crews put together. One of our men that come up from Idaho, out the coast. Of course you couldn't stake him on anything, any part of it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, I see them, I see them log on the coast. But you know, they get a train order sometime, you know, you get a one log and that's a carload. And sometime we put a hundred logs onto the car.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's why they get a coast. Lumber Company used to compete in prices on lumber. Now I think it's different. Nobody knows what the hell it is now. It's up and up and up now.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, that's in 1970.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What happened in 1980? How did you do that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I found the number.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: You found the number?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I didn't go up there. I went back there. I worked a little on board and then the donkey went there. And then left one in the snow and went back up in the spring. Left one out till I got in Oregon. You heard the call of my lumber?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, you know, now we're through. We're done with that again. And now we're in radar and all these other things that none of us old folks never gets up with. It's beyond us. We're so far behind that we never get up any part of it. I don't even try to think about it, what it is now.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I see it. I can see it. I've watched young people and seen some of them at the turbine. And how they live, I don't know. When you see some people now in towns, I don't know how many countries, bad people down in the countries. And if you get in by those hippies and that stuff is, it's terrible. I don't see it. And there's long hair and stuff. I can't agree with that. Do you? Do you like that?

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Well, not personally really.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I can't see a young man. Why in hell he wants to let that goddamn old hair hanging all over him and in the face. And young people now, you know, when you were kids, if we go back that far, you know, you wouldn't have went out like those younger people do now. Then you get a pair of jeans, you know, and they cut them off and fringed them up and covered up a little bit in the middle section and put on patches and they look like hell. And I don't think that's sensible, is it? Really?

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: I don't think that's... Yeah, things in general though don't make the kind of sense that they used to. You know, I think things in the old days, things were easier for people to understand, you know. Things were more straightforward in a way. You had to work, you had to make a living. They were themselves then.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Just like us old fellows is now that we can't, you know, you can't change or learn an old dog new tricks. And us old fellows had pretty hard for us to grasp and to follow what is coming up now because it's changing so fast every day almost. Every year, you know, is a heck of a lot of different from the last one. Each year is getting worse, to my way of thinking. And I can't follow it up, I can't keep up with it. I'm left behind and I'm willing to stay there too. But I see it's the same with us. I can understand how machines work, just the regular machines and how things used to be done, but it gets harder and harder because things, I can't understand the new things very well. Well, that relates to, in all of it, progress. They call it progress. But what's about to become, now we get machines to do every darn thing and hand labor, common labor is out. There isn't any.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: And what are those people going to do?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They can't all live with a pencil. Somebody got to work. I think. You know, you take the farmer. If it wasn't for the farmer, we couldn't even exist. He's feeding us. Because he's got machines now, so he doesn't have to work very much. He can sit in the chair, you know, and go to chair with this one and do as much as a dirty man did 40 years ago. The first combine I saw was a little fordson or a dairy. And I forget the name of it. That fella lived, when he go to airborne. The first fireman on the right side, that was in the 20s, I think. I believe it was in the 20s. When I saw him alone doing all that, he was out there trashing. He was cutting the wheat or whatever it was. And he had a little box on the little fordson tractor. And doing it all by himself. And he had the wagon and the truck sitting up there. And every so often he went and emptied out his, what he had carried, that box he had emptied on. And when he get that truck loaded, he took it over to the elevator and emptied and come back, started over again, filling all by himself. And years ago, you know, when I worked on the farm, they had, when they first started to get the combine big, work with horses. And they had, oh, anyways, up to 20 horses pulling and shoving on it, doing the nursing. And we had that other cutter that they had, you know, made bundles. And we shocked it and run behind it and shocked it, you know. And then stacked it and then they come with the trashing machine and so forth. Now one man can do all of it. You know, the weather is good, he all right, he goes out and never gets a truck load or two, whatever he wants. And if the, and that's in the elevator at night, next day if it's raining all right, he set in the house, that'll the straw dries up again. And then the next day he goes out and do some more of it, all by himself. I guess it's progress, but by golly, you know, the people can't, there is a lot of them left over that hasn't got a job. How they gonna live?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They can't all live on this and give it to me. Rocking chair money, I call it rocking chair money the first, when they come out with the WPA and all that stuff, you know, in the 30s. When they got a check, you know, it's for nothing, doing nothing. Welfare now, it's welfare. And I often wonder, I go into the store once in a while down here and see everybody comes with coupons. How the hell do they get them?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I got to understand how they can get that. Young people, you know, come in there, nobody, they had no money, but they, who's the one who buys cigarettes, they go in their pocketbook, they got money. And a lot of them, I say, when they pay with those coupons, whatever they have, and they change, they get it in money. I guess they won't let them buy beer or anything, they got their money for the beer and cigarettes, I think. They can't buy that. We were well off here in those western states compared to where they were in the eastern, in Illinois and New York State and New Jersey, and in there and in the south, too, they were in the hell of a state compared to us.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: In the Depression?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I got a job in the Depression there in the 30s. I was lucky. I had a job all the way, I was with the, I put in, I put in 10 months with the CCCs in the conservation camps, you know. That was a good thing, I liked it. I was lucky, I got a job and I got, I got $220 a month. Pretty good. Paid 50 cents a day for board. I was up here on the, on the Pondry River for five months at Ruby, oh, at about 50, 60 miles below Newport, towards Medellin, towards Ione.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Between, between Newport and Ione.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What did you do in the camp?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was built on roads. I was superintendent of the camp theory, see what I mean. I was built on roads, I built 20 miles of truck roads in five months. Then the snow got so deep and then I was transferred from there back to, to Herrick, up there by Calder on the San Tio River. I built part of that road up to Avery. I built nine miles of that. From Calder to Fenepe, Fenepe I think. That's stationed on the Milwaukee Road.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, you bet. I liked it, it was good.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Were most of these people in the CCC from the cities?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When I was here in Washington, I was with, oh, let me see now, the regional forester here in Missoula was,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: hmm, oh, I forget his name too. And he was made during the same battalion that I was working in there in France.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Ivan Kelly, wasn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And his brother, he was a sergeant, he was my stable boss. When we were in France, I had 96 head of horses. And we were about 200 rookies in the outfit. No commissioner office with me. I was on disaster service all the while there. That was fun, lots of fun. Worked like hell, all of us. Good bunch. They were from, oh, they were from Idaho and Washington, Montana.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: All of them I think that in that company. Until we had, we got a bunch of New Yorkers towards the end, but they worked more on the sawmill. I didn't get any of them in the woods. In the symbol. But that was fun. Worked like hell, you know, from daylight to dark. Sometime long time after dark.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: You know where we left off when that tape filed up?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You were just going to start talking about Elk River, when you first went out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: You have a question first? I wanted to ask you, when you said that you pile logs, what does that mean that you did?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, you piled logs in Legrand and then you said you piled logs. Oh, I had lumber. Lumber, not logs. Oh, no, not lumber. That lumber, not logs. That's all boards, you see. So you know what they had. The lumber and the dimension is two inches. That was, they called that dimension. And then we had sharp two inches or inch and a half. And then they cut the inch lumber that they graded in one, two, and three. And they had knotty pines, you always hear that. Or you have clear. Not a knot in it. Not a mark on that. That's clear, what they call clear lumber. Like for flooring and so forth, you know. I used to, when I was in there on Elk River, I'd run the log after logging there for half of the time that saw mill ran. Ten years, the last ten years that run, I'd put in all the logs for it. We had three camps. They cut, oh, they cut to one hundred and fifty thousand to the shift, two shifts, three hundred thousand a day and I had to get that much out of the brush, you know, down to the saw rocks.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I found lumber in a pot last summer. We had two inches. They cut one time there when I filed there before I got up to Bovill. We had, they call it mat stock. They cut it two inches out of white pine and they sold it to the diamond mat and they didn't split the board. Some had come four feet and they had to get that on your lap and reach four feet out and hold it and get it on the pile. That was hard work. So we asked them, we quit them, we had the partner one day and we told them we couldn't handle it anymore. We held it down there for a couple of months. We had twenty-one cents a thousand, both feet, you know, for piling it. We had a pilot on crossings, two before crossings, but those big boards, you know, to get up there twenty-four feet high, stood on top of the load, you know, on the track there and get up as much as we could reach and up there and then top it off. And then started a new pile again and at the bottom of there, we couldn't reach it anymore. And we had a pick of the road. Nobody could reach over there and pull them boards to you. So me and the partner, we got ourselves a toy ring with the blacksmith there and he made us a little pick of the road with a spike on it, you know, like this. And a handle with a nut on it so it wouldn't clip and the man on the pile, you know, he reached over and he picked it there and pulled it with that, made a big mark in it. And we had to watch, you know, when the boys come along, we had to hide that. Stay there and grunt and lift, get along the best we could and so one day we, we don't really have to quit. I couldn't hardly hang any overalls, nothing left.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And my partner was the same and he wouldn't let us go and he said, why stay a little while longer?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I said, we stay a little longer again. He said, cut, if you split that and don't, anything above three foot, split the board. And I said, we'll keep on piling. But if you don't do that, we got to quit. We just can't take it. And after that, I guess they had 25 or 30 gangs tried them and nobody had to take that, take that job and then they cut it all down to 12 inch. Usually it was the best we have in the yard then. If we'd have done better, I might have stayed there by the end. We'd have made a lot of money then. At 24 cents a thousand, we could put up a hundred thousand, you know, that'd be $11 a day for each one of us. That was big money, but two dollars a day, day labor. We did five times what we could by the day. You said with three foot long, you had to reach over so far. Wide, wide. Oh, wide. I see. Wide. You see there were two inch thick and four foot wide, you know, and you know, when you stand on the pile, they won't come against you here and reach over and hold it. We don't want them to split up. Anything about three feet, three foot we could handle and reach out. Thirty-six inches we could handle them, you see. But when they come in four and five feet, you know, they would say, possible, you know, they couldn't. That's fantastic lumber to have in your yard. Oh, God. Oh, I tell you, they were heavy. Green lumber, you know, just come out of the woods. Fell them the day before and they come through the summer the next day. I was in Chicago when they cut the big pine. Eleven feet on the stump. Thirty-two thousand feet of log scale in one three, lumber scale in one three.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Where did it come from?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That come from Collins. Just about four miles back of Collins or Bovill, you know, when you go from Bovill to Clarkia. Collins is where you cut across the railroad going to Clarkia. There used to be a halfway house before you had the railroad in. They call it Collins. Their name was Fry. I knew the old folks and I knew Jack Fry. When I left Bovill, he was still there. And one of them had their own house in Moscow. I think that was Sam Fry. I used to eat dinner every once in a while in Moscow. Not on the main street, but the next one over, going towards Blues. Next street over, what the name of it is, I don't know. He had a house there and he served dinners, him and his wife. I was in there at dinner with them several times.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Was that when they were just putting in the railroad?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, yes. They were building the grid that winter. The rail come in the Fourth of July in ten. And I come in there in the first part of November, I think, in nine. Walked in from Bovine.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And the boss come and we were there till in April in the break up. They hauled in a little steam, machine steam hoist, donkey, what we called. Little boiler, one wood, because there was a lot of water and lots of wood. So we would make steam and the pile line. We drew piling all that winter for the mill. So when the rail come in, that whole mill set on pilings. We used thirty-five foot pilings. We had a forty-five foot lead. And drew some of them down thirty-five feet in the ground before they stopped. We drew every pile till they stopped and gave them three, four, eight. We had a, the hammer weighed twenty-six hundred pound and a forty-five foot lead, you know. Lift them up to that twenty-five foot lead and let them fall. In the lead there, you know, it could, and we had a follower that held them in the lead, had the pile. And that, you know, that hit pretty hard. So we drew them down there over thirty feet, every damn pile that was under that mill. And that was hundreds or hundreds, maybe thousands of them. And we drew that all that winter and when they, so when the rail come in there and they could all in lumber, you know. And it was all set to start to build a mill. The foundation is level off those pilings and put the timbers on and build a mill on top. That was the foundation. And the machine trap in the boiler room, we drew piles there, zigzag them two feet apart, you know. So they would put in like this way, they put them two feet apart. And then they dug out and cut them down and they put a cement foundation on top of them pilings. And around the top of it, two foot, and they put the rest of the brick building on top of it. But the piles underneath there was down thirty some feet in the ground. And they were two feet apart. And then they built the brick walls on top of that for the heavy boilers, you know, and that stuff to sit on. And the heavy machine, you know, that big flywheel they had that run the mill, there's a, oh that's it, sixteen foot I think. Went over into the mill to rule all the machinery in there. That belt was ten foot wide I think.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I don't know how long, it was almost a bit about fifty feet long I guess. They were about twenty-five foot over to the mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What was it made out of, the belt?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I could tell you, I don't know. I don't know what it was made out of.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Well what was it like living there in Elk River?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There was nothing there that wouldn't exist. It was too intense, it was too intense. But then just a minute the rail come in, there come the lumber from Portland. Two trains, come up with two trains every day. And they could only haul four cars at the time, four carloads. And they had a locomotive on each end of it. So if they got off the track, you know, some place, one would help the other. Whichever we would take was pretty rough, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: They were dropping them on?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, they got off the track. You bet. One would help the other, you know. And they put in sidings, you know, so they could set out. And if they won't, if the Indian couldn't get on itself, you know, they had to get rid of the cars so the other one could probably pull them on. Pack them up. Yeah, there was a lot of fun them days.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was going to tell you, I walked in there in the break up in the spring. It was in April. And the boys come to me and he said, I was going to be the youngest one in the camp, I think. And he said, I want you to go to Bovill. He said, we'll run our blueprints for the mill site, you see, for us to drive piles on. And he said, we got to get that. So I picked up, after breakfast, you know, I picked up my snow shoes. There was six feet of snow. And places, you know, over there on the first meadow out, or over at the second meadow out, I had to hit the water well deep. And get over there and I made it to Bovill in four hours on snow shoes. And I was there and dried up and had a dinner and the train come in about one o 'clock and I got the mail. And I had supper with them in Elk River at six o 'clock. I had supper with them. A twenty mile hitch way, forty miles. Walking.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Two dollars and I paid for my own dinner.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: That's a little different from today, isn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I often thought about it late three years here. The last ten years, you know, I had a pickup company and drove around. I asked somebody, you go to camp so and so. And we were only three or four miles out from Elk River. And he said, how in the hell am I going to get there? And I said, Jesus Christ, your legs are all right, ain't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: If they ain't, well then you got no business out there. Walk it. Oh, God, no. So most of the time I took, I mean to pick up and do my thing.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now we know they come to walk nothing then. And I used to walk there. I was kind of what they call a walking boss for ten years. And I don't know, I don't think there was a day that I didn't walk thirty miles.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Or more. Nothing to it, you know. Hell, I was slim. I was just like a greyhound. No belly.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was just like an old teamster. I done across there out by a horse ranch. Not horse ranch, but the meadow was in Helmer and Bovell.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What do you call it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know which one you're thinking of.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Is it Shea Meadows?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, three years further up. Hog Meadow. Hog Meadow. Where I camped there, camp five. I was sitting there at the table there. I rode and I was out there in the woods and this fellow was holding his... He says, God dammit, there's something wrong with me. He says, and I don't know. He said, I can't tell. If it's a backache or a bellyache. And by God, I bet you that fellow wasn't that thick too. Tall, slim, thin. I always remember it. He said, I can't tell. He said, whether it's a bellyache or a backache. But he said, it hurt like hell. It's all on the same place.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I can't think of his name now. He was an old teamster. Hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Well, was that the first time that you had a crew of your own? Was that at Elk River?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah. After we got the first crew, I started there was... I was kind of a roust about, bullcoking around the mill and around the town side. And had, oh, I must have had 15, 20 men or something, you know. Anything was to be done, I done it, see, with that little crew of mine. And load lumber or brakes or cement or whatever come in, I done it. And if they had anything digging, you know, to be done, okay, I done that. Then after a total fall there, you know,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: we, they brought up a steam shovel and I dug that pound out in front of the mill. Made a mill pound. And we got little dump cars, small dump cars, they held about two yards or three yards of dirt. Had a little yard pocket on the steam shovel. There was an old boxcar, you know, built in with the hoist in it. And the swing boom on the front of it. Had it on rails, got it in there, we dug that hot pound in there. Then we dug a new channel for the creek past the lumber yard, straight up through there for about a mile. That took the creek meander all through that lumber yard and under the saw mill and everywhere. So we made a new creek channel and that's there yet. That's got us there. Then next spring, next spring then that winter we over the logged a little bit around the town site there. Had a little sleigh hall, three, four, four horse team. Then we hauled logs and dumped them and decked them down in the pond around further a mill. But next spring, you know, the rail was in and then we had to start and get rails out to put a, there was a donkey sitting right there by the powerhouse, machine shop. And pulled off from the side hill up there and pulled the logs right into the mill pound. One little ground lead, donkey 15. And pulled the logs right into the saw mill. That was the closest one there ever was. And then they built the camp down below the dam there, if you've been there, below the mill pound. Before there's a little dam down there. I helped saw the timber there and we cleared that. That's the first we done. Cut trees there and cut them up and burnt the brush. That all cleared that whole pound. That much been a mile and a half I think from the mill down to the dam. And they were building the dam. And then we burnt the brush, you know, and we fell the trees and limbed them and burnt all the brush. And after that first fall there, when the... Two with the dam, you know, closed it.
